<Summary source="https://www.mpamag.com/uk/news/general/reeves-sets-march-2026-spring-forecast/560913">
## Summary: UK Spring Forecast Scheduled for March 2026

**Key Facts:**
- Chancellor Rachel Reeves has scheduled the **Spring Forecast for 3 March 2026**, with the Office for Budget Responsibility (OBR) tasked with preparing an economic and fiscal forecast for that date.
- The forecast will provide an **interim update on the economy and public finances** but will **not** assess the government's performance against its fiscal mandate.
- The government has committed to delivering **only one major fiscal event per year** (the autumn budget); the Spring Statement will be a response to the OBR forecast via a Parliamentary statement.

**Economic Context:**
- Britain's economy **unexpectedly contracted in October**, and the Bank of England forecasts growth is on track to **flatline in Q4**.
- Business groups have warned of a **sharp fall in private sector activity** at the turn of the year, with companies pausing spending and hiring decisions.
- The November autumn budget saw Reeves increase her fiscal headroom to **£22 billion** to guard against future shocks.

**Background Issues:**
- Pre-budget speculation and leaks contributed to **market uncertainty**, including a sell-off in UK government bonds that pushed up borrowing costs.
- The Treasury and OBR are conducting **leak inquiries** related to the autumn budget.

**Relevance to Target Question:** This article is primarily about fiscal/economic policy and does not directly address Keir Starmer's political position or likelihood of leaving office.
</Summary>

<Summary source="https://www.lbc.co.uk/article/what-time-spring-statement-2026-rachel-reeves-5HjdTgy_2/">
## Summary: Rachel Reeves' 2026 Spring Statement (LBC, March 3, 2026)

### Key Facts
- Chancellor Rachel Reeves delivered the government's **2026 Spring Statement on Tuesday, March 3**, likely shortly after midday (no official time confirmed)
- The Office for Budget Responsibility (OBR) published new economic and public finance forecasts following the statement
- **No major announcements** were expected; Whitehall sources described it as a **"non-event"**
- Reports that Reeves would be replaced by a junior minister to deliver the statement were **denied**

### Economic Context
- Reeves received a pre-statement boost: **public sector net borrowing surplus of £30.4 billion in January**
- The surplus was driven by a jump in self-assessed tax payments and debt interest falling to its **lowest level in nearly six years**

### Political Context (Relevant to Starmer Question)
- The article references **Labour's "crippling by-election defeat"**, with Starmer vowing to "fight on" and Angela Rayner calling it a **"wake-up call"** — suggesting ongoing political pressure on the Labour leadership
- A Whitehall source cited concern about **"market instability"** similar to pre-Budget speculation

### Upcoming Events
- **Autumn Budget 2026** expected in October or November 2026

*Note: The article is primarily about the Spring Statement logistics, with only brief references to broader Labour political difficulties.*
</Summary>

<Summary source="https://www.thesun.co.uk/news/38382831/spring-statement-rachel-reeves-speech-time-date/">
## Summary: Spring Statement 2026 Article (The Sun, March 2, 2026)

**Disclaimer:** This article is primarily focused on the logistics and context of the Spring Statement and has limited direct relevance to the question about Keir Starmer's tenure as Prime Minister.

### Key Facts:

- **Chancellor Rachel Reeves** is scheduled to deliver the 2026 Spring Statement in the House of Commons on **Tuesday, March 3, 2026**, expected just after midday.
- The statement is framed as an **interim update rather than a full Budget**, consistent with the government's commitment to one major fiscal event per year (the Autumn Budget).
- The **OBR will provide economic forecasts** but, notably, will *not* formally assess whether the government is meeting its fiscal rules (that assessment now only occurs in autumn).
- Reeves will address the **growth outlook, impact of National Insurance changes, and public service reform strategy**.
- **Recent positive economic indicators** cited:
  - January 2026 recorded a **£30.4 billion budget surplus** — double the same period in 2025.
  - **Inflation dropped to 3%**, potentially opening the door for Bank of England interest rate cuts.

### Relevance to Starmer Question:
The article makes **no direct mention of Keir Starmer's leadership position or any leadership challenges**, though the economic context it describes could indirectly inform assessments of the government's political stability.
</Summary>


<Summary source="https://politicsuk.com/news/kemi-vs-keir-labours-record-in-government-under-pressure-at-pmqs/">
## Summary: PMQs Session – December 10, 2025

**Source:** Politics UK | **Author:** Anna Mardling | **Date:** December 10, 2025

### Key Themes: Leadership Pressure & Government Record

**Badenoch's Attack on Starmer's Leadership:**
- Conservative leader Kemi Badenoch branded Starmer a **"caretaker Prime Minister"**, claiming his weakness has led to frontbench ministers positioning themselves to replace him
- Specifically named **Ed Miliband** (Energy Secretary) as seeking to "recycle himself as leader" and **Wes Streeting** (Health Secretary) as wanting the PM role
- Streeting has **denied** reports of any leadership challenge plans

**Labour's Record Challenged (Badenoch's Claims):**
- Energy bills: Promised -£300 reduction; instead risen by **£187**
- Teachers: Pledged 6,500 new teachers; **400 fewer** teachers since July 2024
- Police: Pledged 13,000 new officers; **1,300 fewer** officers since the election
- NHS: **93,000 appointments lost** to strike action under Labour

**Starmer's Counterarguments:**
- Claimed £150 taken off energy bills (£150 extra for 6 million poorest families)
- Promised **3,000 additional police officers** by end of March 2026
- Repeatedly attacked the Conservative government's record

**EU Customs Union:**
- Lib Dem leader Ed Davey warned Starmer he "would not be standing" at the dispatch box in 12 months without a customs union agreement
- Starmer expressed desire for closer EU relations but prioritized the US trade deal

**Editorial Note:** The article concludes that while Starmer faces significant pressure ahead of **May 2026 local elections**, "for now at least, Starmer is in the clear" regarding his leadership position.
</Summary>

<Summary source="https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2025/12/20/world/politics/starmer-2026-peril/">
## Summary: "How a year of political peril could unfold for Keir Starmer" (*The Japan Times*, December 20, 2025)

**Note:** The article appears to be paywalled, with only a partial excerpt available. The summary below is based solely on the visible content.

### Key Facts & Context:
- Prime Minister Keir Starmer told his Cabinet that **2026 would be the year Britain's fortunes turn around** and Labour gains the upper hand.
- Starmer is described as facing **a battle to save his job** after 18 months in government.
- **Nigel Farage's Reform UK has led national polls since April** (2025), with Labour polling near **historic lows**.

### Sources of Discontent (as described in the article):
- **Flat-lining economic growth** and **high cost of living**
- **Failure to address public concerns about immigration**
- **A series of political scandals and crises** compared to the chaos of the previous Conservative government

### Political Dynamics:
- Starmer has **hemorrhaged support** both within the Labour Party and among the broader electorate.
- Skeptical Labour MPs are described as weighing **whether to oust him** in 2026.

*Disclaimer: The article is truncated due to a paywall, so additional analysis or details from the full piece may not be reflected here.*
</Summary>

<Summary source="https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/2/27/green-party-wins-uk-by-election-in-blow-to-labour-pm-starmer">
## Summary: Green Party Wins Gorton and Denton By-Election

### Key Facts & Statistics
- **Green Party candidate Hannah Spencer** (34-year-old councillor and plumber) won the Gorton and Denton by-election with **40.7% of the vote**
- This was the **Greens' first ever by-election win**, giving them their **5th parliamentary seat**
- **Reform UK finished second**, Labour finished **third**
- Labour had won **more than 50% of the vote** in the same constituency at the 2024 general election
- Gorton and Denton had been considered a **safe Labour seat for nearly a century**
- Reform UK currently holds **8 parliamentary seats**
- The by-election was triggered by former Labour MP Andrew Gwynne standing down for health reasons

### Named Source Opinions
- **Political scientist John Curtice** called the result a **"seismic moment"** signalling that the "future of British politics looks more uncertain than at any stage since the end of World War II" (via Reuters)
- **Labour Party chair Anna Turley** called the result "clearly disappointing"
- **Nigel Farage (Reform UK)** claimed without evidence the result was "a victory for sectarian voting and cheating," predicting "goodbye Starmer" at the upcoming May local elections

### Context Relevant to Starmer's Position
- Starmer had **personally invested political capital** in the race by blocking popular Manchester Mayor **Andy Burnham** (a widely-touted leadership challenger) from standing, and by visiting the constituency before the vote
- Starmer also faces pressure from the **arrest of Peter Mandelson** (his US ambassador appointee) over links to Jeffrey Epstein
- The result adds to **mounting pressure** on Starmer amid Labour's declining poll numbers
</Summary>

Query: UK voting intention poll March 2026 Labour Reform
Here are the relevant news articles:

**YOUGOV: Greens Overtake Labour Into Second Place for First Time**
According to a YouGov poll conducted for the Times and Sky News with fieldwork on Sunday and Monday, March 2026, the Green Party (GRN) has overtaken the Labour Party (LAB) to take second place in voter intention for the first time. The poll results show the Green Party at 21%, a 4-point increase, while Labour stands at 16%, a 2-point decrease. The Conservative Party (CON) is also at 16%, down 2 points, and the Liberal Democrats (LDEM) are at 14%. The Green Party's leader, Polanski, achieved the highest score for his party with YouGov. YouGov attributes the result 'obviously likely driven to a significant extent by the publicity from the Denton & Gorton by-election' and the perception of the Greens as 'a more viable option and less of a wasted vote.' The Conservative Party (CON) remains at 23%.
Original language: en-GB
Publish date: March 03, 2026 01:30 PM
Source:[Guido Fawkes' Blog](https://order-order.com/2026/03/03/yougov-greens-overtake-labour-into-second-place-for-first-time/)

**UK Green Party Surges to Second Place with 21% Support, Overtaking Labour and Conservatives in New Poll**
According to a YouGov survey commissioned by The Times and SkyNews and published on March 03, 2026, the UK Green Party has surged to become the second-largest political force with 21% of voting intention, trailing only the Reform Party (23%) and surpassing both the Labour Party and the Conservative Party, which are tied at 16%. This four-point increase comes one week after Green MP Hannah Spencer won the Gorton and Denton by-election in northern England with 40% of the vote, a result that broke the area's traditional voting patterns. The survey indicates the Greens are now the most popular party among voters under 50, securing 49% support among 18-24 year-olds, 27% among those aged 25-49, and 23% among women. Meanwhile, Prime Minister Keir Starmer's Labour Party support has dropped to a historic low of 16%, matching the Conservative Party's minimum support level, while the Liberal Democrats have fallen to fifth place with 14%. The poll, conducted between Sunday and Monday with a sample of 2,073 people, was analyzed by Anthony Wells, YouGov's Global Director of Politics and Elections, who stated that the Gorton victory 'demonstrated that the Greens can win elections and, as a result, can be perceived as a more viable option and less like a wasted vote,' though he expressed doubt regarding the sustainability of this media momentum, noting the Reform Party's recent decline from 29% in summer and autumn 2025 to 23%. Despite this uncertainty, Green Party leader Zack Polanski, who has led the party since September, confirmed that party membership has tripled from 68,000 to over 200,000 since September, attributing the surge to the Gorton victory and describing the figure as a 'political turning point' against 'managed decline' and 'timid politics'.

**Nightmare for Keir Starmer as Greens overtake Labour in bombshell poll**

This 16% figure represents a two-point decline for both parties and marks the lowest score the pollster has ever recorded for Labour, with the Conservatives sinking to this level for the second time. **Greens leapfrog Labour into second place after historic by-election win, poll shows**
A new YouGov poll conducted for Sky News on Sunday and Monday (March 2026) reveals that the Green Party, led by Zack Polanski, has overtaken the Labour Party to become the second most popular party in the UK with 21% support, following a historic by-election victory in Gorton and Denton. Reform UK leads with 23%, down one point, while Labour and the Conservatives are tied at 16%, both down two points; Labour's 16% represents its lowest score on record. The Liberal Democrats remain at 14%. The Greens are now the most popular party in all age categories under 50, with 49% of 18-24 year olds and 27% of 25-49 year olds backing them. Anthony Wells of YouGov attributes this surge to the Gorton and Denton result, which made the Greens appear a 'viable option' rather than a 'wasted vote'. In the by-election, Green MP Hannah Spencer won with 14,980 votes, beating Reform UK's Matt Goodwin (10,578) and Labour's Angeliki Stogia (9,364), who finished third in a seat Labour previously won with over 50% of the vote. This result marks the highest number of seats the Green Party has ever held in the House of Commons (five MPs). Prime Minister Sir Keir Starmer acknowledged to the Parliamentary Labour Party that 'politics is changing, and it's changing decisively' but maintained his claim that a 'mainstream majority' does not want Nigel Farage or Zack Polanski as prime minister. Professor Sir John Curtice described the polling result as 'seismic', noting that the future of British politics is now 'even more uncertain than it was already'. The poll surveyed 2,073 people.
Original language: en
Publish date: March 03, 2026 09:14 AM
Source:[The Independent](https://www.independent.co.uk/news/uk/politics/green-party-poll-labour-reform-byelection-starmer-polanski-b2930789.html)

**Greens overtake Labour in sensational poll**
In a YouGov poll conducted for Sky News and published on March 02, 2026, the Green Party has leapfrogged the Labour Party to secure second place in UK voting intentions. According to the YouGov weekly voting intention poll, Reform UK leads with 23% (down one point), while the Greens have risen four points to 21%. Labour and the Conservative Party (Tories) are tied at 16% each, both down two points, and the Liberal Democrats remain unchanged at 14%. Anthony Wells, head of global polling at YouGov, attributes the Greens' rise to voters viewing them as a 'more viable option' and 'less of a wasted vote.' The poll highlights that the Greens are now the most popular party in all age categories under 50, with 49% of 18-24 year olds and 27% of 25 to 49-year-olds backing them. Regarding the 2024 general election Labour voters, only 37% intend to vote Labour again, while 25% would now vote Green, 8% would vote Lib Dem, and 20% are undecided or refuse to say. The data has sparked internal debate within Labour, with some factions urging a shift left on issues like immigration to counter the Greens, while others, supporting Sir Keir Starmer, argue for maintaining a tough stance to preserve the electoral coalition. Additionally, the poll indicates the Greens' appeal extends beyond white-collar workers, with significant support from those in 'routine' jobs.

**Reform UK polling lead slashed as Greens overtake Labour**
New polling data from Lord Ashcroft's outlet, conducted between February 19 and February 23, 2026, shows Reform UK's lead has been slashed to just two points at 22%, a significant drop from the high twenties they held for months. According to the data, the Conservatives are two points behind Reform at 20%, while the Greens have overtaken Labour to take third place with 19%, leaving Labour two points behind the Greens at 17%. The article notes this polling occurred before the Greens' by-election win in Gorton and Denton. The report suggests Reform's popularity has peaked and is declining, meaning the party remains well short of a majority and would require a coalition with the Conservatives to form a government, a scenario the Conservatives have shown little interest in. **Farage labelled 'sore loser' as he announces plans to restrict who can vote in elections**
Nigel Farage, leader of the Reform UK party, announced new electoral reform plans on Monday, March 02, 2026, following the party's by-election defeat in Gorton and Denton where the Greens' Hannah Spencer won. According to the plans, only UK citizens (specifically naturalised British citizens) would be permitted to vote in Parliamentary elections, effectively removing voting rights from residents of Commonwealth countries. Additionally, postal voting would be restricted to only elderly and disabled individuals, serving armed forces personnel, and those working overseas during an election. Farage described the current postal voting system as a 'laughing stock' and the ability of non-British citizens to vote as 'absurd'. The announcement follows claims by Reform that 'Muslim sectarian voting' and 'family voting' contributed to the loss, with defeated candidate Matt Goodwin blaming 'Muslim sectarianism' for the defeat. However, the timing of the announcement has drawn sharp criticism; Paul Nowak, chief of the Trades Union Congress, labelled Farage a 'sore loser' and accused him of 'trying to rig the rules' with 'baseless conspiracy theories', comparing him to Donald Trump. Nowak argued that voters in Gorton and Denton rejected Reform's 'nasty and divisive agenda' which includes scrapping employment protections and legalising discrimination. Jon Richards, Unison's assistant general secretary, further questioned Farage's commitment to democracy, noting his lack of interest in overseas influence regarding party funding and suggesting he should have insisted on by-elections whenever a Conservative MP defected to Reform.
Original language: en
Publish date: March 02, 2026 12:44 PM
Source:[The London Economic](https://www.thelondoneconomic.com/politics/farage-labelled-sore-loser-as-he-announces-plans-to-restrict-who-can-vote-in-elections-403665/)

**Elabe Poll: Stability and Security Drive 2026 French Municipal Election Intentions**
With less than two weeks until the first round of the 2026 French municipal elections, an Elabe poll published on Thursday, March 2, 2026, indicates that voter intentions are consolidating around a desire for stability. The survey found that 56% of registered voters are 'absolutely certain' to vote (score 10/10) and 13% are considering it seriously (score 9/10), suggesting a potential turnout higher than the 45% recorded in 2020 during the pandemic, though lower than the approximately 65% seen in previous municipal elections. Turnout certainty varies significantly by age, with 65% of voters aged 65 and older certain to vote compared to only 40% of those aged 18-34. Regarding priorities, security leads at 47%, followed by access to healthcare (41%, up 7 points since January 3), local taxation (35%, down 4 points), and economic development/employment (30%, up 5 points). Territorial divides are evident, with security cited by 50% of voters in large urban areas versus 41% in rural areas, while local taxation is a primary concern for 44% in rural communes versus 31% in large cities. Politically, RN voters prioritize security (71%), Ensemble voters prioritize security (46%) and employment (37%), and NFP voters prioritize health and education. Notably, 58% of voters state their choice will be based primarily on local issues, compared to 7% on national issues. Furthermore, 61% of French people express satisfaction with their current municipality's management, and 60% of voters favor a policy of continuity (up 4 points) over a rupture (40%), a finding that contrasts with a previous Odoxa poll for Public Sénat and La Dépêche which showed 55% favoring a new mayor. Ultimately, voters prioritize an incumbent's honesty and integrity (56%) and managerial efficiency (46%) over political experience (6%) or grand projects (13%).
Original language: fr
Publish date: March 02, 2026 05:31 AM
Source:[Ladepeche.fr](https://www.ladepeche.fr/2026/03/02/municipales-2026-a-moins-de-deux-semaines-du-premier-tour-le-vote-commence-a-se-cristalliser-13248008.php)

**Green Party wins major by-election in northern England city of Manchester: Difference between revisions - Wikinews, the free news source**
In a historic by-election held in the Gorton and Denton constituency of Manchester, northern England, on March 2, 2026, the Green Party secured its first parliamentary victory and first win in the North of England. The candidate, Spencer, won with just under 41% of the vote, establishing a 12% lead over the second-place candidate, Matt Goodwin of Reform UK, who received just under 29%. This result marks Spencer becoming the fifth current Green Party MP. The election served as a significant test for the Labour government, whose candidate, Angeliki Stogia, received 25% of the vote, a sharp drop from the 50% secured in the 2024 general election; the seat, previously considered one of Labour's six safest, had not been lost since 1931. Former Deputy Prime Minister Angela Rayner described the result as a 'wake up call' amidst calls for Prime Minister Keir Starmer to resign, though Starmer stated he had no plans to resign. Matt Goodwin, whose vote share increased by 15% from the 2024 general election, attributed the Green win to 'a coalition of Islamists and woke progressives'. The Conservative Party received just under 2% of the vote, marking their worst by-election result in history and the second time since 1962 they lost their deposit, while the Liberal Democrats received just under 2%, dropping 2% from the previous election. The campaign was marred by allegations of misconduct: Reform UK was acquitted by the High Court regarding a failure to imprint statutory material on a letter from Patricia Clegg, while the Green Party reported the Labour candidate for a campaign van allegedly violating Electoral Commission rules regarding proximity to polling stations. Additionally, the observer group Democracy Volunteers claimed to observe 'family voting' in 68% of stations they monitored, affecting 12% of voters, a practice criminalized by the Ballot Secrecy Act 2023; Manchester City Council and Reform UK leader Nigel Farage disputed the severity, with Farage calling the election a 'victory for sectarian voting and cheating'. The result precedes upcoming May elections where Labour is expected to lose significant seats to Reform UK, the Green Party, Plaid Cymru, and the Scottish National Party.
Original language: en
Publish date: March 02, 2026 03:07 AM
Source:[Wikinews (English)](https://en.wikinews.org/w/index.php)

**Victory in Gorton and Denton is historic for the Greens – and cataclysmic for Britain’s two-party politics**
In the Gorton and Denton byelection held on Thursday, March 1, 2026, Green Party candidate Hannah Spencer, a 34-year-old plumber, secured a historic victory with 40.69% of the vote, defeating Reform UK's Matt Goodwin (28.73%) and Labour's Angeliki Stogia (25.44%). This result marks the sixth-worst byelection defeat in Labour's history, ending the party's 90-year hold on the constituency in Greater Manchester. According to the article, the defeat was exacerbated by Prime Minister Keir Starmer's decision, supported by an eight-to-one vote at the National Executive Committee (NEC), to block the candidacy of Andy Burnham, the Mayor of Greater Manchester, whom the author claims was the most likely candidate to retain the seat. Burnham had previously averaged two-thirds of the vote in mayoral contests and trailed Spencer by 375,000 votes in the 2024 mayoral election. The article notes that voter turnout was identical to the general election. The victory signals a fragmentation of British politics, with the two-party duopoly effectively ending; Labour now faces a significant challenge from the Greens on the left and Reform on the right. The author, Jonathan Tonge, warns that the situation may worsen before it improves, citing upcoming elections in Scotland, Wales, and English local councils within 69 days. The byelection was described as 'toxic,' with accusations of sectarianism against the Greens and racism against Reform's candidate. The article concludes that the first-past-the-post system struggles with this multiparty reality, creating disproportionality between vote shares and representation.
Original language: en
Publish date: March 01, 2026 08:15 PM
Source:[The Conversation - New Zealand](https://theconversation.com/victory-in-gorton-and-denton-is-historic-for-the-greens-and-cataclysmic-for-britains-two-party-politics-277001)

**Non-UK citizens must be banned from voting after by-election 'fraud' Farage says**
Reform UK leader Nigel Farage has claimed that foreign-born voters caused Reform UK to lose the recent Gordon and Denton by-election in Manchester, stating that Reform 'won the Gorton and Denton by-election among British-born voters' and proposing to ban non-UK citizens from voting if he became prime minister. The Green Party candidate Hannah Spencer won the seat with 14,980 votes (41% of the total), while Reform's Matt Goodwin came second with 10,578 votes (28.7%) and Labour's Angeliki Stogia third with 9,364 votes (25.4%). Allegations of voter fraud and sectarianism have emerged, including reports of 'family voting'—an illegal practice where a man enters a booth to instruct family members—which independent observers suggest could have affected up to one in eight votes. Farage claimed that 10% of the electorate were born in Pakistan, with some wards seeing over 20% and one ward reaching 48% of voters born abroad. The Green Party, led by Zack Polanski, is accused of targeting Muslim voters with campaigns focused on Gaza and issuing materials in Urdu. In response to such allegations, a 2023 law was introduced by Conservative peer Lord Haward to criminalize influencing voters in booths, though Haward noted the system 'clearly gone wrong' in this election. Conservative leader Kemi Badenoch blamed Labour for 'harvesting Muslim community bloc votes,' while Greater Manchester Police confirmed they are reviewing a report of 'family voting' made by Farage. The Green Party dismissed the fraud claims as an attempt to 'undermine the democratic result,' and Labour's third-place finish has renewed calls for Sir Keir Starmer to resign. The article was published on March 01, 2026, by The US Sun.
Original language: en
Publish date: March 01, 2026 09:20 AM
Source:[The US Sun](https://www.the-sun.com/news/16018534/non-british-citizens-banned-uk-elections-illegal-voting-farage/)

**'It's electric... there's this real buzz' Hope swells in party on the cusp of power | Wales Online**
Plaid Cymru held its spring conference at the International Convention Centre in Newport on March 1, 2026, just 10 weeks before the Senedd elections, where members expressed unprecedented optimism about ending Labour's 25-year stronghold. According to a recent YouGov poll conducted for ITV Cymru Wales/Barn Cymru, Plaid Cymru projects a 37% vote share, leading Reform UK at 23% and Labour at 10%, though a separate poll by More in Common placed Reform in the lead. Members described the atmosphere as 'electric' and noted the conference was the 'busiest' in the party's history, reflecting a shift in perception where the Welsh electorate and businesses are 'finally taking them seriously.' Long-time member Chris Priest observed that door-to-door interactions have become more positive, with voters either committing to Plaid Cymru or voting to 'keep Reform out.' The party's confidence was bolstered by the recent Caerphilly by-election victory and recent successes in Gorton and Denton. Rhun ap Iorwerth, the party leader, is seen by a More in Common poll as the leader 'most likely to respect the people of Wales,' with 80% of the public believing it is 'time for change.' Despite the hope for a first government term, party members clarified that an independence referendum would not be held immediately, with member Ashley Davies stating, 'Independence is a journey and we have to convince people.'
Original language: en
Publish date: March 01, 2026 02:00 AM
Source:[WalesOnline](https://www.walesonline.co.uk/news/politics/its-electric-theres-real-buzz-33502445)

**Anas Sarwar tries to rally Scottish Labour as poll predicts party could finish fourth at Holyrood election - Daily Record**
Anas Sarwar, leader of Scottish Labour, urged party members to unite ahead of the May 2026 Holyrood election, following a YouGov poll indicating Labour could finish fourth in the Scottish Parliament election. The poll placed Labour third in the constituency vote and fourth in the list vote, with projections suggesting the SNP would win 57 MSPs, Reform UK 22, the Greens 16, Labour 15, the Lib Dems 10, and the Conservatives just nine. Speaking in Paisley, Sarwar framed the election as 'our only chance to end SNP rule,' urging voters to support Labour—even if reluctantly—to prevent the SNP from remaining in power. He warned that voting for Reform UK would only allow the SNP to cling on, saying, 'So, whether you do it with enthusiasm. Whether you do it with anger. Or if you do it by holding your nose, let's come together and back the only party that can get rid of this incompetent SNP government that deserves to lose.' Academic Ailsa Henderson noted that anti-incumbent sentiment, combined with pro-independence voter loyalty to the SNP, lack of Green competition in SNP-held seats, and increased pro-union party competition, could make tactical voting crucial. Fraser McMillian added that Reform UK's vote intention remained consistently high in the teens since early 2025, with no significant tactical voting, positioning the party as the likely official opposition. The analysis suggests Labour faces a 'brutal election' if current polling holds.

**Is the fall in Reform's poll ratings a mirage?**
The article examines whether the apparent decline in Reform Party support in UK polling data is a genuine trend or a 'mirage' caused by shifting levels of respondents answering 'don't know.' While some analysts, including Stephen Bush of the Financial Times, argue that the decline is illusory—attributing it to Labour voters first shifting to 'don't know' and then moving to the Greens or Lib Dems, thereby reducing Reform’s share without a real drop in absolute support—the data from multiple pollsters tell a different story. Using pre-'don't know' removal data from pollsters with fieldwork conducted in 2025, the analysis finds that only More in Common’s data supports the mirage theory. All other pollsters show a genuine decline in Reform’s support. The article also provides updated national voting intention figures, seat projections from MRP models, and leadership ratings. It notes that Conservative support has risen gradually through late 2025 and possibly into early 2026, though the data remains limited. The author invites readers to subscribe for a free 7-day trial to access ten additional polling insights, including findings on public perceptions of net migration, Robert Jenrick’s defection, and MPs’ underestimation of support for environmental policies. A correction is issued for a prior misquote regarding a 2% projection for 2025, with a free annual subscription awarded to the reader who flagged the error.

**What to look out for in 2026**
The final edition of The Week in Polls (TWIP) for 2025 highlights key polling trends and outlooks for 2026 across UK political parties. For the Conservatives, attention will focus on their relative standing versus Reform UK and the sustainability of Kemi Badenoch’s recent polling bounce. Reform UK faces challenges similar to the 1980s Liberal/SDP Alliance, with risks from internal divisions, electoral system inefficiencies, and the need for consistent national vote distribution to translate support into seats. The Liberal Democrats must monitor negative approval ratings for the party and Ed Davey, as low negatives support their 'seats not votes' strategy; council by-election results will be more telling than minor shifts in national polls. The Greens face significant polling volatility, with support ranging from 9% to 19%, and their success depends on whether their left-wing pivot under Zack Polanski is working without losing ground in affluent Conservative areas. For the SNP and Plaid Cymru, dedicated Scottish and Welsh polls are essential for gauging their May 2026 election prospects, as UK-wide polls are unreliable. Labour’s long-term future hinges more on economic data—especially real earnings—than on polling headlines; Keir Starmer’s leadership ratings relative to other Labour figures are critical, as poor performance could trigger a leadership change. The latest national voting intentions show a continued decline in Reform UK’s support, while Find Out Now’s data remains consistent with other polls. MRP seat projections are outdated, and leadership ratings are updated weekly. Subscribers gain access to ten exclusive polling insights, including record economic optimism and the impact of tactical voting. The article concludes with a reminder that economic statistics, not polling fluctuations, will determine Labour’s trajectory in 2026.

**Support For Labour Has Collapsed Among Teachers, Union Poll Shows**
A poll conducted by Deltapoll on behalf of the National Education Union (NEU) and surveyed 3,751 NEU members in England between December 12–15, 2025, reveals a sharp decline in support for the Labour Party among school and college staff. While 60% of respondents voted for Labour in the 2024 general election, only 18% would do so if another election were held tomorrow. Support for the Greens rose from 10% to 23%, making them the most popular political option among NEU members. Support for the Liberal Democrats fell from 9% to 7%, while the Conservatives remained at 4% and Reform UK increased from 3% to 6%. A quarter of respondents said they did not know how they would vote. The findings come amid growing tensions between education unions and the government over teacher pay and reforms to Ofsted. NEU leader Daniel Kebede warned that conditions have worsened since Keir Starmer’s government took office, stating, 'Until the government can accept there's a crisis in education, things are not going to get better for them.' The Department for Education has proposed a 6.5% pay rise over three years, which unions argue does not compensate for real-terms pay cuts. The poll also reflects broader public opinion, with Labour trailing both the Conservatives and Reform UK in voting intention, according to recent surveys. Prime Minister Keir Starmer urged cabinet ministers to ignore polls and 'keep their nerve' during a January 2026 cabinet meeting.

**Andrew Neil's four-word warning to Keir Starmer after nightmare poll**

The Greens rose to 16%, their highest score to date, while Reform UK led with 27%, up one point. The Liberal Democrats remained unchanged at 15%. --- Deep Research Analysis ---
Based on the latest polling data from March 2026, the UK political landscape has experienced a dramatic upheaval with Reform UK holding a narrow lead and the Green Party achieving a historic breakthrough that has pushed Labour into third place for the first time.

**Key Polling Numbers (YouGov, March 3, 2026):**

The most recent YouGov poll conducted on Sunday and Monday, March 1-2, 2026, with a sample of 2,073 adults shows [1][2][5]:

- **Reform UK: 23%** (down 1 point, but leading)
- **Green Party: 21%** (up 4 points, now in second place)
- **Labour: 16%** (down 2 points, tied for third)
- **Conservatives: 16%** (down 2 points, tied for third)
- **Liberal Democrats: 14%** [1][5]

This represents Labour's lowest polling score on record with this pollster, matching the Conservatives at a historic minimum support level [4][5].

**Context: The Gorton and Denton Effect**

These results follow the Green Party's historic by-election victory in Gorton and Denton (Greater Manchester) on February 26, 2026, where Green candidate Hannah Spencer won with 40.69% of the vote (14,980 votes), defeating Reform UK's Matt Goodwin (28.73%) and Labour's Angeliki Stogia (25.32%)—a seat Labour had held for nearly 100 years and won with over 50% in the 2024 general election [5][12]. YouGov's Global Director of Politics and Elections, Anthony Wells, attributed the Green surge directly to this victory, stating it demonstrated the Greens are "a more viable option and less of a wasted vote" [1][2][3].

**Demographic Breakdown**

The Greens have become the most popular party among all voters under 50 [2][3][5]:
- **Ages 18-24:** 49% support for Greens
- **Ages 25-49:** 27% support for Greens
- **Ages 50+:** Reform UK leads (29-33%)

Meanwhile, Reform UK maintains strong support among older voters, with 33% of those aged 65+ [12].

**Additional Polling Context**

It's worth noting that different polling methodologies show slight variations. Lord Ashcroft's poll conducted February 19-23, 2026 (before the Gorton and Denton result) showed Reform UK at 22%, Conservatives at 20%, Greens at 19%, and Labour at 17% [7]. POLITICO's "Poll of Polls" aggregation shows Reform UK at 26%, Conservatives 17%, Labour 16%, and Greens 15% [3], suggesting the YouGov figures may represent peak Green momentum immediately following their by-election triumph.

**Party Membership Surge**

Green Party leader Zack Polanski, who took over in September 2025, has overseen a dramatic expansion in membership from 68,000 to over 200,000—tripling in six months [2]. Professor Sir John Curtice described the polling result as "seismic," noting that "the future of British politics is now even more uncertain than it was already" [5].

Prime Minister Keir Starmer has acknowledged to his Parliamentary Labour Party that "politics is changing, and it's changing decisively," though he maintains that a "mainstream majority" does not want Nigel Farage or Zack Polanski as prime minister [5]. The 25% of 2024 Labour voters who have now switched to the Greens represents a significant electoral threat to Starmer's leadership [3].
